sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Tendinopathy and other overload syndromes usually reflect a mismatch between tissue capacity and repeated load. Effective care focuses on load modification, progressive strengthening, and ruling out inflammatory or serious alternate causes.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain that warms up, worsens after load, or is worse the next day
- Tenderness at tendon or load-bearing structures
- Stiffness after inactivity
- Reduced tolerance for repeated, overhead, gripping, jumping, or climbing tasks

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Do not assume every chronic tendon-region pain is tendinopathy; consider bursitis, nerve pain, fracture, inflammatory arthritis, or systemic illness
- Complete unloading often reduces capacity further
- Workplace repetition, vibration, awkward posture, and inadequate recovery are common contributors

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify the aggravating load pattern, work demand, and current functional tolerance
- Reduce provocative volume or intensity without complete unnecessary rest
- Use simple pain relief and basic support strategies within scope
- Escalate if swelling, heat, rupture concern, or inflammatory/systemic features are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Start a staged strengthening and reloading plan matched to irritability and site
- Address technique, recovery, sleep, and load progression rather than chasing imaging findings alone
- Educate that discomfort during rehab may occur but progressive deterioration is not the goal
- Refer for imaging or specialist review when rupture, inflammatory disease, or persistent severe disability is suspected

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Progress should be judged by function, load tolerance, and symptom trend over time, not by pain elimination at rest alone
